title: Run a Stage 8 Science Fair
overview: Bring together everything from Stage 8 Science to run a science fair that teaches visitors the most important ideas from this stage through real investigations.

project.drivingQuestion: How can we use the science from Stage 8 to run an accurate, safe and exciting science fair for our school or community?
project.finalProduct: Create a science-fair plan with a live investigation, labelled displays, a safety checklist, a visitor survey with a data display, and a short explanation of your scientific decisions.

stages.title: 1. Foundations display
stages.prompt: Choose the two most important ideas from Units 1, 2, 3. Build a display that teaches each idea with a model or diagram, an example and a check question.
stages.evidence: Two labelled displays with models, examples and check questions

stages.title: 2. Live investigation
stages.prompt: Pick one investigation from Units 4, 5, 6 and run it live: state your hypothesis, follow the method, record results and explain your conclusion.
stages.evidence: A completed investigation with hypothesis, method, results and conclusion

stages.title: 3. Connections wall
stages.prompt: Create a connections wall that links ideas from Units 7, 8, 9 to everyday life, with at least three labelled connections.
stages.evidence: A connections wall with three labelled links to daily life

stages.title: 4. Present and explain
stages.prompt: Present your science fair. Explain at least three scientific choices, check that your conclusions match your evidence and identify one improvement you would make.
stages.evidence: Spoken, written or recorded scientific explanation

evidenceChecklist: A safety checklist for every activity
A connections wall with three links
A survey with organised data display

rubric.secure: Observations, measurements, models and conclusions are accurate and checked.
Hypotheses, fair testing, recording and conclusions follow the scientific method.
Labels, diagrams, tables or charts make the science visible.
Decisions are explained using appropriate science words and evidence.

questions.question: Asexual reproduction in plants produces offspring that are:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: identical to the parent. Asexual reproduction uses one parent and no joining of sex cells, so offspring are clones.

questions.question: The male part of a flower is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: stamen. The stamen (anther and filament) is the male part; remember 'stamen = man'.

questions.question: The male sex cell is called the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: sperm cell. The sperm cell is the male sex cell, made in the testes; the egg cell is the female sex cell.

questions.question: Egg cells are made and released by the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: ovaries. The ovaries store and release egg cells and make the hormone oestrogen.

questions.question: Density is best described as:
options: the mass packed into each unit of volume,
questions.answer: the mass packed into each unit of volume,
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the mass packed into each unit of volume Density compares mass with the volume it occupies.

questions.question: The formula for density is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: D = m / V Density is mass divided by volume.

questions.question: The smallest particle of an element is called an:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: atom. An atom is the smallest particle of an element that still behaves as that element.

questions.question: About how many different elements are there?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: 118. There are about 118 known elements, around 90 of them found naturally.

questions.question: A substance made of only one type of particle is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a pure substance - it is made of only one type of particle.

questions.question: In salty water, the salt is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: solute - the salt is the substance that dissolves.

questions.question: Which of these is a luminous object?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the sun. A luminous object makes its own light; the moon, a mirror and a mango only reflect light.

questions.question: Light travels in:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: straight lines. This is shown by sharp shadows and narrow beams.

questions.question: Which nutrient is the body's main and fastest source of energy?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: carbohydrate - carbohydrates give the fastest, main energy supply.

questions.question: Which food is the best source of protein for growth?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: fish - fish is rich in protein for growth and repair; the others are mainly energy or empty.

questions.question: A natural solid usually made of a mixture of minerals is called a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: rock - a rock is a natural solid usually made of a mixture of minerals.

questions.question: Rock formed when molten rock cools and hardens is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: igneous - it forms when molten rock cools and hardens.

questions.question: An electric current is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the flow of electric charge - that is the definition of a current.

questions.question: Current is measured in:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: amperes - current is measured in amps (A); volts measure voltage and ohms measure resistance.
